description: Trigger discrimination: checks on production DATA CONTENT (this skill) vs system telemetry implementation (observability-operator), test-data design (test-data-architect), LLM output shape validation (structured-output-validator), and one-time migration verification (data-migration-runbook-author).

cases.prompt: Design checks so we know when our orders dataset is stale, missing rows, full of duplicates, or referencing customers that don't exist — and decide what happens to rows that fail.
cases.reason: Freshness, completeness, uniqueness, and referential integrity are assertions on data CONTENT, and the failure-action question (block/quarantine/alert) is this skill's core vocabulary. No alert wiring, test fixtures, or LLM outputs are in scope.

cases.prompt: Wire up the alerts: page when pipeline job duration exceeds an hour, dashboard the consumer lag, and add a health check for the transform service.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — job duration, lag, and health checks are SYSTEM signals, and the verbs are implementation (wire, dashboard, add). Even for a check this skill designed, the alert-platform wiring is observability-operator's manual-invocation surface.
cases.reason: Nothing asserts on data content; the request is telemetry implementation for pipeline infrastructure.

cases.prompt: Our integration tests keep flaking because seeded rows collide across parallel workers — design deterministic fixtures and per-worker isolation.
cases.reason: The data is TEST data: fixtures, seeds, and parallel isolation. This skill watches production datasets; test-data determinism is a different discipline with its own owner.

cases.prompt: The model sometimes returns malformed JSON missing required fields — validate its responses against the schema before we act on them.
cases.reason: Validating an LLM response's shape at generation time is structured-output-validator's boundary. Data-quality monitoring asserts on datasets in pipelines and stores, not on a model's per-response envelope.

cases.prompt: For next week's backfill, write the per-batch verification queries — row counts and checksums between old and new tables — and the abort criteria.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — parity checks during a PLANNED one-time move are runbook verification gates. The monitor designer owns STANDING checks that outlive the move; if dual-writes persist after cutover, a standing reconciliation check is where this skill picks up.
cases.reason: One-time, per-batch verification with abort criteria is execution safety for a specific operation, not an ongoing monitor.
